title: Shortest unique prefix
description: Find shortest unique prefix for each word

examples.explanation: Processing 'dog': Check prefix 'd' - not a prefix of any other word → output 'd'. Processing 'cat': Check prefix 'c' - not a prefix of any other word → output 'c'. Processing 'apple': Check prefix 'a' - 'apricot' also starts with 'a' → extend. Check prefix 'ap' - 'apricot' also starts with 'ap' → extend. Check prefix 'app' - not a prefix of any other word → output 'app'. Processing 'apricot': Check prefix 'a' - 'apple' also starts with 'a' → extend. Check prefix 'ap' - 'apple' also starts with 'ap' → extend. Check prefix 'apr' - not a prefix of any other word → output 'apr'. Processing 'fish': Check prefix 'f' - not a prefix of any other word → output 'f'.

Processing 'zebra': Check prefix 'z' - not a prefix of any other word → output 'z'. Processing 'dog': Check prefix 'd' - 'duck' and 'dove' also start with 'd' → extend. Check prefix 'do' - 'duck' and 'dove' don't start with 'do' → output 'do'. Wait, need to check all: 'duck' starts with 'du' not 'do', 'dove' starts with 'dov' not 'do' → actually 'do' is unique! Correction: output 'do'. Processing 'duck': Check prefix 'd' - 'dog' and 'dove' also start with 'd' → extend. Check prefix 'du' - not a prefix of any other word → output 'du'. Processing 'dove': Check prefix 'd' - 'dog' and 'duck' also start with 'd' → extend. Check prefix 'do' - 'dog' starts with 'do' → extend. Check prefix 'dov' - not a prefix of any other word → output 'dov'. Final correction: output is ['z','do','du','dov'].

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n*m²)
approaches.summary: For each word, compare it with every other word to find the shortest prefix that is unique. This involves checking prefixes of increasing length until a mismatch is found.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n² * m) where n is number of words and m is average word length

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Shortest Unique Prefix
ladders.desc: Complete implementation finding shortest unique prefix for each word by comparing with all other words.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force solution to the shortest unique prefix problem. The intuition is straightforward: for each word in the list, we need to find the shortest prefix that doesn't appear as a prefix of any other word. We achieve this by comparing each word against every other word. For a given word, we start with its first character and check if this prefix appears at the start of any other word. If it does, we extend the prefix by one character and repeat until we find a prefix that's unique. This approach works because we're exhaustively checking all possibilities - if a unique prefix exists, we'll eventually find it when our prefix becomes long enough to differentiate from all other words. The algorithm proceeds as follows: 1) Initialize an empty result list. 2) For each word in the input list: a) Start with prefix length = 1. b) While True: i) Extract current prefix = word[0:prefix_len]. ii) Check if this prefix is a prefix of any other word. iii) If NOT found in any other word, add prefix to results and break. iv) Otherwise, increment prefix_len and continue. c) If we reach prefix_len > len(word), the entire word becomes the prefix (this happens when words are identical). 3) Return result list. Time complexity is O(n² * m) where n is number of words and m is average word length - we have nested loops (n words × n comparisons) and each comparison can go up to m characters. Space complexity is O(1) excluding output storage. This connects directly to L1's prefix checking subroutine - L0 orchestrates the complete solution using L1's functionality. Common mistakes include: 1) Not handling duplicate words correctly (should return the full word as prefix). 2) Off-by-one errors in string slicing. 3) Inefficient re-checking of prefixes. Avoid these by carefully testing edge cases and understanding that when prefix_len exceeds word length, the word itself must be the answer. Real code pattern: def shortest_unique_prefix(words): result = [] for i, word in enumerate(words): prefix_len = 1 while True: prefix = word[:prefix_len] unique = True for j, other in enumerate(words): if i != j and other.startswith(prefix): unique = False break if unique or prefix_len > len(word): break prefix_len += 1 result.append(prefix) return result

examples.trace: Processing 'dog': Check prefix 'd' - not a prefix of any other word → output 'd'. Processing 'cat': Check prefix 'c' - not a prefix of any other word → output 'c'. Processing 'apple': Check prefix 'a' - 'apricot' also starts with 'a' → extend. Check prefix 'ap' - 'apricot' also starts with 'ap' → extend. Check prefix 'app' - not a prefix of any other word → output 'app'. Processing 'apricot': Check prefix 'a' - 'apple' also starts with 'a' → extend. Check prefix 'ap' - 'apple' also starts with 'ap' → extend. Check prefix 'apr' - not a prefix of any other word → output 'apr'. Processing 'fish': Check prefix 'f' - not a prefix of any other word → output 'f'.

Processing 'zebra': Check prefix 'z' - not a prefix of any other word → output 'z'. Processing 'dog': Check prefix 'd' - 'duck' and 'dove' also start with 'd' → extend. Check prefix 'do' - 'duck' and 'dove' don't start with 'do' → output 'do'. Wait, need to check all: 'duck' starts with 'du' not 'do', 'dove' starts with 'dov' not 'do' → actually 'do' is unique! Correction: output 'do'. Processing 'duck': Check prefix 'd' - 'dog' and 'dove' also start with 'd' → extend. Check prefix 'du' - not a prefix of any other word → output 'du'. Processing 'dove': Check prefix 'd' - 'dog' and 'duck' also start with 'd' → extend. Check prefix 'do' - 'dog' starts with 'do' → extend. Check prefix 'dov' - not a prefix of any other word → output 'dov'. Final correction: output is ['z','do','du','dov'].

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Prefix Existence Check
ladders.desc: Check if a given prefix appears at the start of any word in a list (excluding a specified word).
ladders.explanation: This is the most critical helper function in the brute force approach. The intuition is that to determine if a prefix is unique for a particular word, we need to verify that no other word in the list begins with that same prefix. This subroutine answers the question: 'Does this prefix appear as the starting substring of any word other than the current one?' The algorithm is: 1) Accept parameters: words list, current word index, prefix string. 2) Iterate through all words in the list. 3) For each word at position j: a) Skip if j equals the current word index (we're comparing against other words only). b) Check if words[j] starts with the given prefix. c) If true, return False immediately (prefix is not unique). 4) If loop completes without finding any match, return True (prefix is unique). Time complexity is O(n * m) in worst case where n is number of words and m is prefix length (though startswith() typically has O(m) complexity). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L0 as the core checking mechanism - L0 calls this function repeatedly with increasing prefix lengths. Common mistakes: 1) Forgetting to exclude the current word from comparison. 2) Not handling empty strings correctly. 3) Assuming case sensitivity incorrectly. Avoid by clearly defining comparison rules and testing edge cases. Real code pattern: def prefix_exists(words, current_idx, prefix): for j, word in enumerate(words): if j != current_idx and word.startswith(prefix): return True return False

examples.trace: Check word at index 0: skip (current word). Check word at index 1: "apricot" starts with "app"? No → continue. Check word at index 2: "banana" starts with "app"? No → continue. Loop complete, no matches found → return False (prefix doesn't exist in other words).
Check word at index 0: "cat" starts with "cat"? Yes → return True immediately (prefix exists in another word).

ladders.title: Core Logic — Iterative Prefix Extension
ladders.desc: For a given word, systematically try longer prefixes until finding one that's unique.
ladders.explanation: This represents the central algorithmic insight of the brute force approach. The intuition is that uniqueness is monotonic with respect to prefix length - if a prefix of length k is not unique, we need to try a longer prefix. We start with the smallest possible prefix (first character) and gradually extend it character by character until we either find a unique prefix or exhaust the word. This works because: 1) Shorter prefixes are more likely to collide with other words. 2) Each additional character reduces the probability of collision. 3) Eventually, we'll either find a distinguishing prefix or use the entire word (which must be unique if all words are identical). The algorithm: 1) Start with prefix_len = 1. 2) While True: a) Extract current prefix = word[0:prefix_len]. b) Check if this prefix is unique (not a prefix of any other word). c) If unique OR prefix_len > len(word): i) This prefix is our answer. ii) Break from loop. d) Else: increment prefix_len and continue. Time complexity for this logic on a single word is O(n * m²) in worst case where we check up to m prefixes and each check takes O(n * m). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L1 as the driver that uses the prefix checking subroutine - L2 decides when to stop extending based on L1's results. Common mistakes: 1) Infinite loop when all words are identical (need the prefix_len > len(word) condition). 2) Not considering that the entire word might be needed as prefix. 3) Off-by-one errors in string indexing. Avoid by carefully defining termination conditions and testing with duplicate words. Real code pattern: def find_prefix_for_word(words, current_idx): word = words[current_idx] prefix_len = 1 while True: prefix = word[:prefix_len] if is_unique_prefix(words, current_idx, prefix) or prefix_len > len(word): return prefix prefix_len += 1

examples.trace: Word = "apple". Try prefix_len=1: prefix="a" - check uniqueness: "apricot" starts with "a" → not unique. Try prefix_len=2: prefix="ap" - check uniqueness: "apricot" starts with "ap" → not unique. Try prefix_len=3: prefix="app" - check uniqueness: "apricot" starts with "app"? No → unique found → return "app".

Word = "cat". Try prefix_len=1: prefix="c" - check uniqueness: other "cat" starts with "c" → not unique. Try prefix_len=2: prefix="ca" - check uniqueness: other "cat" starts with "ca" → not unique. Try prefix_len=3: prefix="cat" - check uniqueness: other "cat" starts with "cat" → not unique. Try prefix_len=4: prefix_len > len(word) condition triggers → return "cat" (entire word).

ladders.title: Building Block — String Prefix Checking
ladders.desc: Determine if one string starts with another string (prefix relationship).
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental string operation needed for the brute force approach. The intuition is simple: string A is a prefix of string B if the first k characters of B exactly match all characters of A, where k is the length of A. We need to verify this character-by-character relationship. The algorithm: 1) If prefix length > word length, return False immediately (a string cannot start with a longer prefix). 2) Iterate i from 0 to len(prefix)-1: a) Compare prefix[i] with word[i]. b) If any character differs, return False. 3) If all characters match, return True. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the length of the prefix. Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L2 as the basic comparison operation - L2 uses this to check if a candidate prefix appears at the start of other words. Common mistakes: 1) Not checking length condition first (important for efficiency and correctness). 2) Off-by-one errors in loop bounds. 3) Case sensitivity issues. Avoid by carefully implementing the length check and remembering that string indexing starts at 0. Real code pattern: def starts_with(word, prefix): if len(prefix) > len(word): return False for i in range(len(prefix)): if word[i] != prefix[i]: return False return True

examples.trace: Length check: len("ap")=2 ≤ len("apricot")=7 → continue. Compare index 0: 'a' == 'a' → match. Compare index 1: 'p' == 'p' → match. All characters matched → return True.

Length check: len("band")=4 ≤ len("banana")=6 → continue. Compare index 0: 'b' == 'b' → match. Compare index 1: 'a' == 'a' → match. Compare index 2: 'n' == 'n' → match. Compare index 3: 'd' != 'a' → mismatch → return False.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Slicing/Substring Extraction
ladders.desc: Extract a prefix (first k characters) from a string.
ladders.explanation: This is the most fundamental operation in the brute force approach - extracting progressively longer prefixes from a word. The intuition is that we need to examine the word's beginning portion, starting from the first character and extending to various lengths. In programming terms, this is string slicing: given a string S and an integer k, we want the substring consisting of the first k characters of S. The algorithm is straightforward: 1) Ensure k is non-negative. 2) If k is 0, return empty string. 3) If k > len(S), typically we return the entire string (though in our use case, we control k to not exceed length). 4) Otherwise, return S[0:k] (characters from index 0 up to but not including index k). Time complexity is O(k) for creating the new substring. Space complexity is O(k) for the new string. This connects to L3 as the source of prefixes to check - L4 provides the candidate prefixes that L3 then compares against other words. Common mistakes: 1) Off-by-one errors (remember slicing is exclusive of the end index). 2) Not handling edge cases like k=0 or k > length. 3) Confusing string indexing (0-based). Avoid by practicing with examples and remembering Python/JavaScript slicing semantics. Real code pattern: def get_prefix(word, k): if k <= 0: return '' if k >= len(word): return word return word[:k]

examples.trace: Word has 9 characters. Extract first 3 characters: indices 0,1,2 → 'a','l','g' → result "alg".
k=5 > len(word)=4 → return entire word "test".

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Strings as Character Sequences
ladders.desc: Understanding that strings are ordered collections of characters with indexing and length properties.
ladders.explanation: This is the absolute foundation for all string manipulation in the brute force approach. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we can examine or compare them one character at a time by referring to their positions. In programming, strings are typically indexed starting from 0, so the first character is at position 0, second at position 1, etc. The basic operations are: 1) Get length of string: len(s) returns number of characters. 2) Access character at index i: s[i] returns the character. 3) Important: attempting to access beyond valid indices causes an error. This foundational concept enables all the higher-level operations: slicing (L4) uses character access to extract substrings, prefix checking (L3) compares characters at same positions, and the full algorithm (L0) relies on this for all comparisons. Time complexity for accessing a character is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to L4 as the building block for slicing - L5's character access enables L4 to extract prefixes. Common beginner mistakes: 1) Thinking indexing starts at 1 (it starts at 0). 2) Accessing out-of-bounds indices. 3) Confusing string length with last index (last index is len(s)-1). Avoid by practicing with small examples and remembering zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: # Basic operations s = "hello" length = len(s) # 5 first_char = s[0] # 'h' second_char = s[1] # 'e' last_char = s[4] # 'o' OR s[-1]

examples.trace: String "cat" has length 3. Index 0: character 'c'. Index 1: character 'a'. Index 2: character 't'.

examples.input: String="hello", get length and first/last characters
examples.trace: len("hello") = 5. First character: index 0 = 'h'. Last character: index 4 = 'o' (since 5-1=4).

testCases.input: String="abc", get last character using len-1

approaches.name: HashMap O(n*m)
approaches.summary: Store all possible prefixes of each word in a hashmap with their counts. Then, for each word, find the shortest prefix with a count of 1. This avoids pairwise comparisons by aggregating prefix frequencies.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * m) where n is number of words and m is average word length
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n * m) for storing all prefixes

ladders.title: Full Problem — Shortest Unique Prefix using HashMap
ladders.desc: Complete solution finding shortest unique prefix for each word using prefix frequency counting
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that instead of comparing each word against all others (which would be O(n²)), we can precompute all possible prefixes and count their frequencies. A prefix becomes unique when it appears exactly once across all words. For each word, we generate all prefixes starting from the first character, and the first prefix with count 1 is our answer. This works because if a prefix appears only once, no other word shares that prefix, making it uniquely identifiable. The algorithm has three phases: 1) Build a frequency map by iterating through all words and all their prefixes, incrementing counts. 2) For each word, iterate through its prefixes in increasing length order until finding one with count 1. 3) Return the list of shortest unique prefixes. Time complexity is O(n*m) where n is number of words and m is average word length, as we process each character of each word twice (once for building map, once for finding prefix). Space complexity is O(n*m) to store all prefixes in the hashmap. This connects to L1 by using the prefix frequency counting subroutine as the core mechanism. Common mistakes include: not handling empty input, forgetting to include the full word as a prefix, or stopping at the first prefix without checking if it's unique. Real code pattern: def shortest_unique_prefix(words): prefix_count = {} for word in words: for i in range(1, len(word)+1): prefix = word[:i]; prefix_count[prefix] = prefix_count.get(prefix, 0) + 1 result = [] for word in words: for i in range(1, len(word)+1): prefix = word[:i]; if prefix_count[prefix] == 1: result.append(prefix); break return result

examples.trace: Step 1: Build prefix frequency map. For 'dog': prefixes 'd', 'do', 'dog' → counts: d:1, do:1, dog:1. For 'cat': 'c', 'ca', 'cat' → c:1, ca:1, cat:1. For 'apple': 'a', 'ap', 'app', 'appl', 'apple' → a:2, ap:2, app:1, appl:1, apple:1. For 'apricot': 'a', 'ap', 'apr', 'apri', 'apric', 'aprico', 'apricot' → a:3, ap:3, apr:1, apri:1, apric:1, aprico:1, apricot:1. For 'fish': 'f', 'fi', 'fis', 'fish' → f:1, fi:1, fis:1, fish:1. Step 2: Find shortest unique prefix for each word. 'dog': check 'd' count=1 → unique, output 'd'. 'cat': 'c' count=1 → unique, output 'c'. 'apple': 'a' count=3 (shared), 'ap' count=3 (shared), 'app' count=1 → unique, output 'app'. 'apricot': 'a' count=3, 'ap' count=3, 'apr' count=1 → unique, output 'apr'. 'fish': 'f' count=1 → unique, output 'f'.

Step 1: Build prefix frequency map. 'zebra': 'z', 'ze', 'zeb', 'zebr', 'zebra' → z:1, ze:1, zeb:1, zebr:1, zebra:1. 'dog': 'd', 'do', 'dog' → d:3, do:1, dog:1. 'duck': 'd', 'du', 'duc', 'duck' → d:3, du:2, duc:1, duck:1. 'dove': 'd', 'do', 'dov', 'dove' → d:3, do:1, dov:1, dove:1. Step 2: Find shortest unique prefix. 'zebra': 'z' count=1 → 'z'. 'dog': 'd' count=3 (shared), 'do' count=1 → 'dog' (since 'do' is unique, but we need to check: actually 'do' appears for 'dog' and 'dove', so count=2? Wait, let's recount: 'dog' prefixes: d, do, dog. 'dove' prefixes: d, do, dov, dove. So 'do' appears twice → count=2. Correction: 'dog': 'd' count=3, 'do' count=2, 'dog' count=1 → 'dog'. 'duck': 'd' count=3, 'du' count=2 (shared with 'dove'? 'dove' has 'do', not 'du'), actually 'du' only from 'duck' and 'dove'? 'dove' has 'd', 'do', 'dov', 'dove' → no 'du'. So 'du' count=1? Wait, 'duck' and 'dove' don't share 'du'. Let's rebuild map carefully: All prefixes: zebra: z,ze,zeb,zebr,zebra; dog: d,do,dog; duck: d,du,duc,duck; dove: d,do,dov,dove. Counts: z:1, ze:1, zeb:1, zebr:1, zebra:1; d:4 (from dog, duck, dove, and also from zebra? no), actually d appears in dog, duck, dove → count=3; do:2 (dog, dove); dog:1; du:1 (only duck); duc:1; duck:1; dov:1; dove:1. So 'duck': check 'd' count=3, 'du' count=1 → unique, output 'du'. 'dove': 'd' count=3, 'do' count=2, 'dov' count=1 → unique, output 'dov'.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Prefix Frequency Counting
ladders.desc: Build a hashmap counting occurrences of all prefixes across words
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that by counting how many times each prefix appears, we can later identify which prefixes are unique (count = 1). This avoids the need for pairwise comparisons. The algorithm iterates through each word and generates all possible prefixes from length 1 to the full word length, incrementing their counts in a hashmap. For example, for word 'cat', we add 'c', 'ca', and 'cat'. The time complexity is O(n*m) where n is number of words and m is average length, as we process each character once per prefix generation. Space complexity is O(n*m) to store all prefixes. This feeds into L0 by providing the frequency data needed to find unique prefixes. Common mistakes include: using a set instead of counting duplicates, or not including the full word as a prefix. Real code pattern: def build_prefix_count(words): count = {}; for word in words: for i in range(1, len(word)+1): prefix = word[:i]; count[prefix] = count.get(prefix, 0) + 1; return count

examples.trace: Step 1: Process 'ab'. Prefixes: 'a' → count becomes {'a': 1}. 'ab' → {'a': 1, 'ab': 1}. Step 2: Process 'ac'. Prefixes: 'a' → increment 'a' count to 2 → {'a': 2, 'ab': 1}. 'ac' → add 'ac' with count 1 → {'a': 2, 'ab': 1, 'ac': 1}.

Step 1: 'test' prefixes: 't' → {'t': 1}; 'te' → {'t': 1, 'te': 1}; 'tes' → {'t': 1, 'te': 1, 'tes': 1}; 'test' → {'t': 1, 'te': 1, 'tes': 1, 'test': 1}. Step 2: 'team' prefixes: 't' → increment to 2 → {'t': 2, 'te': 1, 'tes': 1, 'test': 1}; 'te' → increment to 2 → {'t': 2, 'te': 2, 'tes': 1, 'test': 1}; 'tea' → add {'t': 2, 'te': 2, 'tes': 1, 'test': 1, 'tea': 1}; 'team' → add {'t': 2, 'te': 2, 'tes': 1, 'test': 1, 'tea': 1, 'team': 1}.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Finding First Unique Prefix
ladders.desc: Given a word and prefix counts, find the shortest prefix with count 1
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that uniqueness is determined solely by the count in the frequency map. For a given word, we check its prefixes in order from shortest to longest. The first prefix with count 1 is guaranteed to be the shortest unique prefix because if a shorter prefix had count 1, we would have taken it earlier. The algorithm takes a word and the prefix count map, iterates i from 1 to word length, generates prefix = word[:i], and returns the first prefix where count[prefix] == 1. Time complexity is O(m) for a single word of length m, as we may check up to m prefixes. Space is O(1) extra. This connects to L1 by using the count map built earlier. Common mistakes include: not handling the case where no unique prefix exists (should return full word), or starting iteration from 0 instead of 1. Real code pattern: def find_shortest_unique(word, count): for i in range(1, len(word)+1): prefix = word[:i]; if count.get(prefix, 0) == 1: return prefix; return word

examples.trace: Step 1: i=1, prefix='a', count=2 → not unique. Step 2: i=2, prefix='ap', count=2 → not unique. Step 3: i=3, prefix='app', count=1 → unique, return 'app'.
Step 1: i=1, prefix='z', count=1 → unique, return 'z'.

ladders.title: Building Block — Generating All Prefixes of a String
ladders.desc: Produce all substrings starting from the first character
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that a prefix is defined as a substring that starts at index 0 and goes up to some length i. For a string of length L, there are exactly L prefixes (including the full string). For example, 'cat' has prefixes 'c', 'ca', 'cat'. This is fundamental because we need to consider all possible prefixes to find the shortest unique one. The algorithm takes a string s, iterates i from 1 to len(s), and yields s[0:i]. Time complexity is O(L²) if storing all prefixes, but O(L) if just processing them. Space is O(L) to store all prefixes. This connects to L2 by providing the prefixes to check for uniqueness. Common mistakes include: including the empty string as a prefix (usually not needed), or using wrong indices. Real code pattern: def get_prefixes(s): return [s[:i] for i in range(1, len(s)+1)]

examples.trace: Step 1: i=1 → 'h'. Step 2: i=2 → 'he'. Step 3: i=3 → 'hel'. Step 4: i=4 → 'hell'. Step 5: i=5 → 'hello'.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Slicing
ladders.desc: Extract a substring from start index to end index
ladders.explanation: String slicing is the operation of taking a contiguous part of a string. In Python-like syntax, s[start:end] returns characters from index start up to but not including end. For prefixes, we always start at 0, so s[0:i] gives the first i characters. The intuition is that this allows us to efficiently generate prefixes without manual concatenation. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the length of the slice, as it creates a new string. Space is O(k). This connects to L3 by being the mechanism to get each prefix. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors (using s[0:i] where i is 0-based vs 1-based), or misunderstanding that slicing returns a new string. Real code pattern: prefix = s[0:3]  # gets first 3 chars

examples.trace: Step 1: Identify indices: start=0, end=3. Step 2: Extract characters at positions 0,1,2 → 'a', 'l', 'g'. Step 3: Combine into new string 'alg'.
Step 1: start=0, end=4. Step 2: Positions 0,1,2,3 → 'd','a','t','a'. Step 3: Combine into 'data'.

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Hashmap/Dictionary Basics
ladders.desc: Store and retrieve key-value pairs with O(1) average time.
ladders.explanation: A hashmap (or dictionary) is a data structure that maps keys to values. The intuition is that it allows fast lookup, insertion, and deletion by using a hash function to compute an index into an array. In this problem, we use it to store prefixes as keys and their counts as values. For example, after processing 'cat', the map has {'c':1, 'ca':1, 'cat':1}. Operations include: put(key, value) to store, get(key) to retrieve, and incrementing counts. Time complexity is O(1) on average for these operations. Space is O(n) for n entries. This connects to L4 by providing the storage for prefix counts. Common mistakes include: not handling missing keys (use get with default), or assuming order. Real code pattern: count = {}; count['key'] = count.get('key', 0) + 1

examples.input: Initialize map, add key 'a' with value 1, then increment 'a' to 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with empty map {}. Step 2: Add 'a' with value 1 → {'a': 1}. Step 3: Retrieve current value of 'a' (1), add 1 to get 2, store {'a': 2}.

examples.input: Map has {'x':3, 'y':1}. Add key 'z' with value 5, then increment 'x' by 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Start with {'x':3, 'y':1}. Step 2: Add 'z':5 → {'x':3, 'y':1, 'z':5}. Step 3: Increment 'x' by 2: get current 3, add 2 to get 5, store {'x':5, 'y':1, 'z':5}.

approaches.name: Trie O(n*m)
approaches.summary: Build a trie from all words, tracking node visit counts. Traverse each word in the trie to find the first node where the count is 1, indicating a unique prefix. This leverages the trie's prefix-sharing property.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n * m) where n is number of words and m is average word length
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n * m) for the trie structure

ladders.title: Full Problem — Shortest Unique Prefix using Trie
ladders.desc: Complete solution to find shortest unique prefix for each word using a trie with node visit counts.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is that a trie naturally organizes words by their prefixes, allowing us to efficiently track how many words share each prefix. By storing a count at each trie node representing how many words pass through that node, we can traverse each word in the trie and find the first node where the count is exactly 1. This node corresponds to the shortest prefix that uniquely identifies that word among all input words. The algorithm works because if multiple words share a prefix, their paths in the trie will overlap, and nodes along the shared path will have counts greater than 1. Only when we reach a point where the path diverges (or a word ends) do we get a count of 1. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Define a TrieNode class with children dictionary and count integer. 2) Build the trie by inserting all words, incrementing count at each node visited. 3) For each word, traverse the trie from root, collecting characters until we reach a node with count 1. 4) Return the collected prefix. Time complexity is O(n*m) where n is number of words and m is average word length, as we process each character of each word twice (once during insertion, once during query). Space complexity is O(n*m) for storing the trie structure. This connects directly to the full problem by providing the complete implementation. Common mistakes include: not resetting the traversal for each word, forgetting to handle empty input, and incorrectly updating counts during insertion. The real code pattern involves careful trie construction and systematic traversal.

examples.trace: Step 1: Build trie. Insert 'dog': root->d(count=1)->o(count=1)->g(count=1). Insert 'cat': root->c(count=1)->a(count=1)->t(count=1). Insert 'apple': root->a(count=1)->p(count=1)->p(count=1)->l(count=1)->e(count=1). Insert 'apricot': root->a(count=2)->p(count=2)->r(count=1)->i(count=1)->c(count=1)->o(count=1)->t(count=1). Insert 'fish': root->f(count=1)->i(count=1)->s(count=1)->h(count=1). Step 2: Find prefixes. For 'dog': traverse d(count=1) -> prefix='d'. For 'cat': traverse c(count=1) -> prefix='c'. For 'apple': traverse a(count=2) -> p(count=2) -> p(count=1) -> prefix='app'. For 'apricot': traverse a(count=2) -> p(count=2) -> r(count=1) -> prefix='apr'. For 'fish': traverse f(count=1) -> prefix='f'.

Step 1: Build trie. Insert 'zebra': root->z(count=1)->e(count=1)->b(count=1)->r(count=1)->a(count=1). Insert 'dog': root->d(count=1)->o(count=1)->g(count=1). Insert 'duck': root->d(count=2)->u(count=1)->c(count=1)->k(count=1). Insert 'dove': root->d(count=3)->o(count=2)->v(count=1)->e(count=1). Step 2: Find prefixes. For 'zebra': traverse z(count=1) -> prefix='z'. For 'dog': traverse d(count=3) -> o(count=2) -> g(count=1) -> prefix='dog'. For 'duck': traverse d(count=3) -> u(count=1) -> prefix='du'. For 'dove': traverse d(count=3) -> o(count=2) -> v(count=1) -> prefix='dov'.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Trie Traversal for Unique Prefix
ladders.desc: Traverse a pre-built trie to find the shortest unique prefix for a given word.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that once we have a trie built with node counts, finding the shortest unique prefix for a word becomes a simple traversal problem. We start at the root of the trie and follow the path corresponding to the word's characters. At each node we visit, we check the count value. The moment we encounter a node with count equal to 1, we know that this node represents a prefix that is unique to this word (no other word shares this exact path up to this point). We collect all characters from the root up to this node to form the shortest unique prefix. If we reach the end of the word without finding a count of 1, then the entire word itself is its own shortest unique prefix (this happens when words are prefixes of each other, like 'code' and 'coder'). The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Start at root node with empty prefix string. 2) For each character in the word: a) Move to the child node corresponding to the character. b) Append character to prefix. c) If current node's count == 1, return prefix. 3) If loop completes without finding count 1, return the entire word. Time complexity is O(m) where m is the word length, as we traverse at most m nodes. Space complexity is O(1) additional space beyond the trie. This feeds into the full problem by providing the query mechanism after trie construction. Common mistakes include: not handling the case where no unique prefix exists within the word, incorrectly checking count before moving to child node, and not properly initializing the traversal. The real code pattern involves a while or for loop with early return when count condition is met.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at root, prefix=''. Step 2: Process 'a': move to a-node (count=2), prefix='a'. Count=2 ≠ 1, continue. Step 3: Process 'p': move to p-node (count=2), prefix='ap'. Count=2 ≠ 1, continue. Step 4: Process 'p': move to p-node (count=1), prefix='app'. Count=1 == 1, return 'app'.
Step 1: Start at root, prefix=''. Step 2: Process 'd': move to d-node (count=1), prefix='d'. Count=1 == 1, return 'd'.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Tracking Node Visit Counts in Trie
ladders.desc: Maintain count at each trie node representing how many words pass through that node.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that by augmenting the standard trie structure with a count field at each node, we can track prefix sharing information efficiently. Each node in the trie represents a prefix (the path from root to that node). The count field stores how many words in the input list have that prefix. When we insert a word into the trie, we increment the count at every node we visit along the insertion path. This means: 1) The root has count equal to total number of words (all words pass through root). 2) If two words share a common prefix, all nodes along their common path will have count ≥ 2. 3) When paths diverge, nodes after the divergence point will have count = 1 for each unique branch. The intuition is that count values directly encode prefix uniqueness information. A count of 1 means exactly one word has this exact prefix, making it a candidate for shortest unique prefix. The step-by-step algorithm for maintaining counts: 1) Start at root node. 2) For each character in word to insert: a) If child node for character doesn't exist, create it with count=0. b) Move to child node. c) Increment count at current node. 3) After processing all characters, the final node (representing the complete word) also gets its count incremented. Time complexity per insertion is O(m) where m is word length. Space complexity for the count field is O(1) per node. This technique feeds into the key sub-routine by providing the count data needed for traversal decisions. Common mistakes include: forgetting to increment count at root, not initializing new nodes with count=0 before incrementing, and incorrectly handling duplicate words. The real code pattern involves a simple increment operation during trie insertion.

examples.trace: Step 1: Insert 'cat'. Start at root: increment root.count to 1. Process 'c': create c-node with count=0, move to it, increment to 1. Process 'a': create a-node with count=0, move to it, increment to 1. Process 't': create t-node with count=0, move to it, increment to 1. Step 2: Insert 'car'. Start at root: increment root.count to 2. Process 'c': c-node exists, move to it, increment count to 2. Process 'a': a-node exists, move to it, increment count to 2. Process 'r': create r-node with count=0, move to it, increment to 1.

Step 1: Insert first 'a'. root.count=1, a-node created with count=1. Step 2: Insert second 'a'. root.count=2, a-node count becomes 2. Step 3: Insert 'ab'. root.count=3, a-node count becomes 3, create b-node with count=1.

ladders.title: Building Block — Trie Data Structure Implementation
ladders.desc: Implement a basic trie (prefix tree) data structure with insertion capability.
ladders.explanation: A trie (pronounced 'try') is a tree-like data structure that stores strings by breaking them into characters. Each node represents a single character, and paths from root to nodes represent strings or prefixes. The intuition is that tries optimize prefix-based operations by sharing common prefixes among different strings. For example, words 'cat' and 'car' share the prefix 'ca', so in a trie they would share the first two nodes (c and a), then diverge at the third node (t vs r). This prefix sharing makes tries space-efficient for storing many words with common prefixes and enables fast prefix queries. The step-by-step implementation: 1) Define a TrieNode class with: a) children dictionary mapping character to child TrieNode. b) Optional end_of_word boolean flag (though for our problem we'll use count instead). 2) Insert method: Start at root. For each character in word: if character not in current node's children, create new TrieNode. Move to child node. After processing all characters, mark end_of_word as True. 3) The trie itself maintains a root TrieNode. Time complexity for insertion is O(m) where m is word length. Space complexity is O(n*m) in worst case when no prefixes are shared, but typically much less due to prefix sharing. This building block feeds into the core logic by providing the underlying structure on which we can add count tracking. Common mistakes include: not using dictionary for children (array is also possible but less flexible), forgetting to handle empty strings, and not properly initializing nodes. The real code pattern involves recursive or iterative traversal with node creation as needed.

examples.trace: Step 1: Insert 'cat'. Start at root. 'c' not in root.children, create node for 'c'. Move to 'c' node. 'a' not in c.children, create node for 'a'. Move to 'a' node. 't' not in a.children, create node for 't'. Move to 't' node. Mark end_of_word=True at t-node. Step 2: Insert 'dog'. Start at root. 'd' not in root.children, create node for 'd'. Move to 'd' node. 'o' not in d.children, create node for 'o'. Move to 'o' node. 'g' not in o.children, create node for 'g'. Move to 'g' node. Mark end_of_word=True at g-node.

Step 1: Insert 'bat'. Create path root->b->a->t, mark end at t. Step 2: Insert 'batman'. Traverse existing path root->b->a->t. 'm' not in t.children, create m-node. Continue creating a-node, n-node. Mark end at n-node.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Character-by-Character String Traversal
ladders.desc: Process a string one character at a time, performing operations based on each character.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves iterating through a string character by character and performing some action for each character. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and many string algorithms (including trie operations) require examining each character in order. By processing characters sequentially from first to last, we can build up results incrementally or make decisions at each step. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Initialize an index or pointer to the start of the string (position 0). 2) Use a loop (for or while) to iterate through all characters: a) Access the current character using string indexing. b) Perform the desired operation (e.g., update a data structure, check a condition). c) Move to next character by incrementing index. 3) After loop completes, handle any post-processing. Time complexity is O(n) where n is string length, as we visit each character once. Space complexity is typically O(1) if not storing additional data. This operation feeds into the trie implementation as both insertion and query require character-by-character processing. Common mistakes include: off-by-one errors in loop bounds, forgetting to handle empty strings, and not using proper string indexing methods. The real code pattern involves simple iteration constructs like 'for char in word:' in Python.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize: word='hello', i=0. Step 2: i=0, char='h', print 'h'. Step 3: i=1, char='e', print 'e'. Step 4: i=2, char='l', print 'l'. Step 5: i=3, char='l', print 'l'. Step 6: i=4, char='o', print 'o'. Step 7: Loop ends as i=5 exceeds length.

examples.input: String: 'cat', Operation: build list of ASCII values
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize: word='cat', result=[], i=0. Step 2: i=0, char='c', ord('c')=99, append 99 to result. Step 3: i=1, char='a', ord('a')=97, append 97 to result. Step 4: i=2, char='t', ord('t')=116, append 116 to result. Step 5: Loop ends, return result.

ladders.title: L5: Concept Foundation — Strings as Character Sequences
ladders.desc: Understanding that strings are ordered collections of characters with indexing and length properties.
ladders.explanation: Strings are fundamental data structures that represent text as sequences of characters. The intuition is that just as arrays store elements at positions, strings store characters at positions (called indices). Each character in a string has a position starting from 0 (first character) up to length-1 (last character). This ordered nature allows us to access individual characters, iterate through them, and perform operations based on their positions. Key properties: 1) Length: number of characters in the string. 2) Indexing: accessing character at specific position using notation like s[i]. 3) Immutability: in many languages, strings cannot be changed after creation (though new strings can be created). 4) Characters: the individual elements, which may be letters, digits, symbols, etc. Understanding strings as sequences is crucial because the shortest unique prefix problem fundamentally involves processing strings character by character. When we work with words like 'dog' or 'cat', we need to examine 'd', then 'o', then 'g' in that exact order. This foundation feeds into character-by-character traversal by providing the basic understanding of how strings are structured. Common beginner mistakes include: confusing 0-based indexing with 1-based, trying to modify strings directly instead of creating new ones, and not handling empty strings properly. The real code pattern involves using string methods and properties like len(s), s[0], and for loops.

examples.output: Length: 5, Characters: h(0), e(1), l(2), l(3), o(4)
examples.trace: Step 1: The string 'hello' has 5 characters. Step 2: Position 0 contains 'h'. Step 3: Position 1 contains 'e'. Step 4: Position 2 contains 'l'. Step 5: Position 3 contains 'l'. Step 6: Position 4 contains 'o'. Step 7: There is no position 5 as length is 5.
Step 1: The string 'a' has 1 character. Step 2: Position 0 contains 'a'. Step 3: Position 1 does not exist as length is 1.

testCases.input: String: 'racecar', Is it same forwards and backwards?
